title: Strangers, Different Colors, Same Game
trend: World Cup 2026 semifinal week (July 14-15, verified 2026-07-08 via ESPN/Olympics.com/Al Jazeera/Sky Sports). Rides the verified wholesome fan-connection theme (supporters from opposing countries swapping shirts and becoming friends despite a language barrier) reported by Euronews and Yahoo/creators.yahoo.com — theme only, no real name, photo, country, team, crest, or footage referenced in copy, per trend-jacking-playbook.md rule 2.
hook: This World Cup, strangers keep becoming friends over a game they didn't even play in. You don't need a World Cup for that.

body.caption:
This World Cup, some of the best stories aren't from the pitch. Fans who don't even speak the same language have been swapping shirts, sharing meals, and becoming friends — just because they showed up for the same game, from different sides.

Quick reminder while we have your attention: KeepItUp is a free app that helps you find a small local game or activity near you — five-a-side, a run, padel, anything — and meet the people who show up too. Some people find a date. Some find a running group. Some just want someone to play with on a Tuesday. It all starts the same way the shirt-swaps did: two strangers, one game.

You don't need a World Cup for that feeling. You need one game, this week. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: Poster-branded single image, real photo, no real teams/kits/crests. Photo: pexels-33920366-flag-football.jpg (adults playing flag football, golden hour, high energy — our own casual pickup game, not tournament footage). KeepItUp mark top-left, small. Eyebrow (neon-green): 'KEEPITUP: FREE LOCAL GAMES TO MEET PEOPLE.' Headline block (off-white on anthracite scrim, lower third): 'STRANGERS. DIFFERENT COLORS. SAME GAME. THAT'S HOW IT STARTS EVERY TIME.' Footer band: KeepItUp wordmark + 'open worldwide, early access.' Planned composite: b12p1-strangers-same-game.png. Asset below points at the base real photo pending that bake step.

title: Monday, The World Stops Watching Together
trend: World Cup 2026 final, Sunday July 19 (verified 2026-07-08 via ESPN/Olympics.com/Al Jazeera/Sky Sports). Factual public-schedule reference only, no marks/trophy/crests/team names — the angle is the shared-watching feeling ending, not the match itself.
hook: The final was Sunday. Monday, most of that shared-watching feeling goes away — unless you make your own version of it, every week.

body.caption:
Sunday, the World Cup ends. A month of strangers filling bars together, cheering with people they'd never met before kickoff, wraps up with one final whistle.

Here's the part nobody plans for: on Monday, most of that goes away. No more built-in excuse to gather around a screen with people who somehow felt like friends by injury time.

KeepItUp is a free app for the version of that feeling that doesn't need a World Cup: a small local game or activity near you, every week, with people who actually show up. Some come for a date, some for a running group, some just want a Tuesday five-a-side. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: We Just Opened
hook: Today's the day. KeepItUp is open — worldwide, starting right now. No hype, just an honest new option.

body.caption:
Today's the day. KeepItUp is open — worldwide, starting right now.

Here's the honest version of what that means: no huge launch numbers to brag about, no manufactured hype. Just an app that helps you find a small local game or activity near you, ask to join, and see who shows up. Could be a date. Could be your new running group. Could be someone to play chess with on Thursdays.

We built it because the usual way — endless swiping, or paying to stand around at a mixer with a drink — wasn't working for us either. It's free. It's live. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: How KeepItUp Works (Opening Day Poster)
hook: We're live. Here's exactly how KeepItUp works, in one picture.

body.caption: For everyone just finding us this week: here's exactly how KeepItUp works. Find a small local game or activity near you. Ask to join. The host says yes and you get the exact time and place. Show up and play. That's it — no long forms, no endless scrolling, no fee to try it. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: The Longest-Living People All Have One Thing
hook: In Okinawa, the people who live longest all belong to a small group of friends who meet for life. That's not an accident.

body.caption:
One thing keeps showing up in the research on the world's longest-lived people. In Okinawa, Japan — one of the places researcher Dan Buettner's Blue Zones project studied — many people belong to a moai: a small group of about five to twenty friends who commit to meeting regularly, for life. Some of these groups have stayed together for over 90 years. Buettner and the Blue Zones team link that kind of steady, small-group friendship to some of the strongest health and longevity outcomes they found anywhere (Blue Zones, 'Moai — This Tradition Is Why Okinawan People Live Better, Longer,' 2018).

You don't need to live in Okinawa. You need a standing group. KeepItUp helps you find one — set your profile to friendship, and find the same few people for your regular game, every week. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: Sport Has The Power To Unite
hook: "Sport has the power to unite people in a way that little else does." — Nelson Mandela, 2000.

body.caption:
In 2000, at the first Laureus World Sports Awards, Nelson Mandela said: 'Sport has the power to change the world. It has the power to inspire. It has the power to unite people in a way that little else does.' He wasn't talking about one big tournament — he'd spent his life watching a shared game bring together people who had every reason not to trust each other.

That's the same small idea behind KeepItUp, just at a much smaller scale: a local game is one of the easiest ways for two strangers to end up on the same side. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: The Plan That Wasn't
hook: "We should all get a game together sometime" — said in January. Still "sometime." It's July.

body.caption:
You know this plan. Someone says 'we should all get a volleyball game together sometime' in a group chat, everyone reacts with a thumbs up, and six months later it's still 'sometime.' Nobody's plan, so nobody owns it, so it never happens.

KeepItUp is a free app that fixes the part that actually breaks: one person hosts a game, sets a real time and place, and people nearby can ask to join. No more group-chat archaeology. Just an actual game, with an actual host, on an actual day. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: Why-us pain post, mutation of the approved 'The Match That Wasn't' pattern applied to a group-plan instead of a dating match. Poster-branded, real photo. Photo: pexels-12169344-volleyball-laugh.jpg (two friends laughing mid beach-volleyball game — the payoff moment, the plan that finally happened). KeepItUp mark top-left, small. Two stacked text blocks over the photo (top third and middle third, off-white on anthracite scrim): top — '"WE SHOULD ALL GET A GAME TOGETHER SOMETIME." — SAID IN JANUARY.' middle — 'STILL "SOMETIME." IT'S JULY.' Bottom subhead (smaller, neon-green rule above it): 'SOME PLANS NEED A HOST, A TIME, AND A PLACE — NOT ANOTHER GROUP CHAT.' Footer band: wordmark + 'open worldwide, early access.' Planned composite: b12p7-the-plan-that-wasnt.png.

title: The Manifesto, Vol. 2: More Choices Made It Worse
hook: More options did not make dating better. It made it worse. Here's the psychology, and what we built instead.

slides: PART 2 OF THE MANIFESTO. MORE OPTIONS DID NOT MAKE DATING BETTER. IT MADE IT WORSE.

PSYCHOLOGIST BARRY SCHWARTZ CALLED IT THE PARADOX OF CHOICE: PAST A CERTAIN POINT, MORE OPTIONS MAKE A DECISION HARDER, NOT EASIER. Schwartz, The Paradox of Choice, 2004. (Some follow-up studies get mixed results — the idea isn't gospel. It just matches what an endless swipe deck feels like.)
AN ENDLESS DECK OF STRANGERS ISN'T FREEDOM. IT'S PARALYSIS, DESIGNED TO KEEP YOU SCROLLING.
SO WE BUILT THE OPPOSITE. NOT A DECK OF FACES. ONE SMALL GAME, NEAR YOU, WITH A REAL START TIME.
FEWER CHOICES. MORE SHOWING UP. KEEPITUP — OPEN WORLDWIDE, EARLY ACCESS, LINK IN BIO.
body.caption:
Here's a theory we build the whole product around. Psychologist Barry Schwartz's well-known 'paradox of choice' idea says more choice doesn't make people happier — past a certain point, it makes decisions harder and satisfaction lower (Schwartz, The Paradox of Choice, 2004; worth saying honestly that later studies get mixed results, so we're not calling it gospel — just naming what an endless swipe deck feels like). A dating app with an infinite deck of strangers is that paradox by design.

KeepItUp does the opposite on purpose: instead of an endless scroll, you see one small local game with a real time and place, ask to join, and the host says yes or no. Fewer choices. More actual showing up. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: We Replaced Small Talk With A Small Game
hook: Skip "so, what do you do for fun?" Just go do the fun thing. Twenty minutes tells you more than any bio does.

body.caption:
KeepItUp is a free app for finding a small local game or activity near you and meeting the people who show up. Here's the part we didn't expect to love this much: it skips the worst question in dating and making friends — 'so, what do you do for fun?'

You don't have to describe it. You just do it, with the person, in real time, and find out in twenty minutes whether you actually get along. Way more accurate than a bio. Way less awkward than small talk. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

Subhead: 'Twenty minutes tells you more than any bio does.' Footer band: wordmark + 'open worldwide, early access.' Planned composite: b12p9-small-talk-small-game.png.

title: Finish The Sentence
hook: Finish the sentence: "I'd actually show up to a game near me if ___." Comment your answer.

body.caption:
Finish the sentence: 'I'd actually show up to a local game near me if ___.' Comment your answer — we're genuinely reading these to figure out what would get more people off the couch and into a small local game.

Quick reminder for anyone new here: KeepItUp is a free app that helps you find a small local game or activity near you — for dating, friends, or just a crew. You ask to join, the host says yes, you show up. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
